posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Props to the new TWA hot-shot, William Kelley. He really nailed this wine, recommended it to me several months ago.

It’s a gem.

Decanted for two hours in perfectly cool, 60-degree, if not down-right nippy, Florida weather. Pretty tight red berries, cigar, old barn plank and florals on the nose, the less tolerant of you out there might find a hint of brett. Loving this Bordeaux musk, a really great old school perfume here. The palate still sports its youth, taking a lot of time and air to open up. Tight, tart core of red spectrum fruits, ranging to some darks and plums later into the evening. Dry earth and ground cover (sous bois), tobacco leaf, savory herbs. A transparent texture to this wine, all the elements well-delineated. Excellent acidic structure to this wine, a hint of citrus fruits, with firm but softening tannins. A throw-back wine to a style from the past. No polish, no gloss, just a rustic elegance that I adore in a wine. It seems to me that, if you love Chinon and Sociando-Mallet, you will really love this wine.

(93 pts.)

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Good call - this is hard to find but well worth looking for and I get the Chinon comparison completely. It’s old school Margaux in all the right ways.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Thanks. I made the Sociando reference a little in jest, a comment about the throw-back style, but truthfully, this wine is more elegant and not a powerful as Sociando, and did not exhibit much of the greeen notes that put off so many to Sociando. But they both share a stylistic similarity in their deference to traditionalism.

A side note, I understand that this wine sees no new oak.

Robert Alfert:

I threw in the last line so Neal won’t buy this wine and subject me to the humiliation of 0-3.

It worked!

posts.name: Corey Nøɤܝ©Ʞ
posts.cooked: Did you find at Chambers Street? Looks like they have several vintages in stock.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked: Yes - Sociando is a different beast, which personally I’m not a great fan of! I’m sure you’re right about the oak - J-Pierre Boyer is an oddball who steadfastly ignores everyone and everything. Neal Martin (unsurprisingly) gave the 1970 a 90pt review last year, but otherwise the wines are never tasted and never written about.

posts.name: Anders G
posts.cooked:
Robert Alfert:

It’s a gem.

Indeed. Love the freshness and the old-school- or rather “vintage”- style of these wines.
AFAIK this is completely aged in concrete tanks, I don’t know of any other Chateau that does this any more.
I went through half a case of the 2000. The 1998 didn’t hold up so well, so I figured I better finish of the 2000 as well. Reading your note I guess I should have kept a few bottles, but they were pretty darn tasty, so no regrets. The 2005 is quite decent as well and I have a bottle in the pipeline.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Julian Marshall:

Yes - Sociando is a different beast, which personally I’m not a great fan of! I’m sure you’re right about the oak - J-Pierre Boyer is an oddball who steadfastly ignores everyone and everything. Neal Martin (unsurprisingly) gave the 1970 a 90pt review last year, but otherwise the wines are never tasted and never written about.

I opened that 1970 with Neal! I think it would have shown a couple of points better had I not decanted it.

I did a brief piece on Bel Air Marquis d’Aligre in Noble Rot last year. But most of the information online about the Château is in French.

The maturation is four years in concrete FWIW.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Anders G:
Robert Alfert:

It’s a gem.

Indeed. Love the freshness and the old-school- or rather “vintage”- style of these wines.
AFAIK this is completely aged in concrete tanks, I don’t know of any other Chateau that does this any more.
I went through half a case of the 2000. The 1998 didn’t hold up so well, so I figured I better finish of the 2000 as well. Reading your note I guess I should have kept a few bottles, but they were pretty darn tasty, so no regrets. The 2005 is quite decent as well and I have a bottle in the pipeline.

It is my understanding that the juice sees 6 months of old barrels right after fermentation, and then is transfered to concrete for 2-3 years of aging.

posts.name: Julian Marshall

Thanks for putting me right - it’s nice to see that there are other writers who think outside the box.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Apparently Levenberg and I are on the same drinking schedule. Enjoying yet another 2000 BAMA right now, But check out his wonderful note on Cellartracker from a few days ago.

This is probably the best showing that I have had of this wine, and I’ve probably had anywhere between seven or eight bottles. The wine is so perfectly in the zone right now. Hitting its mature stride and destined to run for a long time. Such a melange of flavors packed into a lithe physique. Blood orange so palpable. Keeps this wine fresh while still displaying mature old world data points.

Really outstanding.

(94 pts.)

posts.name: Arjan Stavast
posts.cooked:
Popped one of these yesterday and it is in absolutely great shape now indeed! One of the best BAMAs so far for me, just behind the ‘71 I had last year.
Straight after popping the cork there was a lot of fine red fruit, which evolved into some underwood, herbs, leather and brambles with more air.

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Another thumbs up - I had one last night - it really is coming on well, perfectly balanced, with as Robert said four years ago, something very Chinon-like about it. There’s a freshness that other Margaux 2000s are lacking. It was my first for two years and I’ll be in no hurry to go back - it’s delicious but I can see it getting even better in a few more years. Over here at least, the 00 is no longer available, except at auction, all the more reason not to hurry to drink mine.

I’m curious as to whether M. Boyer will ever produce another vintage - anyone know? The last put on the market was the 2012. I’m pretty sure there were wines made in 2014, 2015 and 2016 as I seem to remember that Neal Martin tasted them from vats during his visit a few years ago, but I have never seen any.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’m curious as to whether M. Boyer will ever produce another vintage - anyone know? The last put on the market was the 2012. I’m pretty sure there were wines made in 2014, 2015 and 2016 as I seem to remember that Neal Martin tasted them from vats during his visit a few years ago, but I have never seen any.

I’ll hopefully be finding out after harvest! Monsieur Boyer had some health problems earlier this year but is, I am told, back on track.

posts.name: Brian G r a f s t r o m
posts.cooked:
2000 Château Bel Air-Marquis d’Aligre - France, Bordeaux, Médoc, Margaux (12/5/2022)
– double-decanted approx. 75 min. before initial taste –
– tasted non-blind over 4.25 hrs –

NOSE: expressive and high-toned; aged red fruits; autumnal; light hint of cumin seed; started presenting as as a bit sweetish about 3.75 hrs after opening; hint of strong mint.

BODY: medium bodied; garnet color of medium to medium-deep depth, with slight-to-medium bricking throughout.

TASTE: aged red fruits; tanned leather; medium+ acidity; light bloomy rind cheese funk; as far as my preferences go, I don’t anticipate this will improve from here, so Drink Now.

50, 5, 12, 16, 7 = (90 pts.)
posts.display_username: Brian G r a f s t r o m

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
“with as Robert said four years ago, something very Chinon-like about it.”

Is that a positive or a negative for a Margaux?

posts.name: Julian Marshall
posts.cooked:
Howard_Cooper:

“with as Robert said four years ago, something very Chinon-like about it.”

Is that a positive or a negative for a Margaux?

Well for Robert and myself, this is something very positive, because as I scribbled in the next sentence, “There’s a freshness that other Margaux 2000s are lacking” - so for me at least, much better than Rauzan-Ségla for example. But not everyone would agree.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Julian_Marshall:
Howard_Cooper:

“with as Robert said four years ago, something very Chinon-like about it.”

Is that a positive or a negative for a Margaux?

Well for Robert and myself, this is something very positive, because as I scribbled in the next sentence, “There’s a freshness that other Margaux 2000s are lacking” - so for me at least, much better than Rauzan-Ségla for example. But not everyone would agree.

Well, I was teasing you two a bit, but wouldn’t BAMA cost a lot more than most Chinons?

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked: Depends, some Loire CFs have gotten pricey. BAMA is definitely Bordeaux, the Chinon reference does not mean it tastes like a Chinon, it suggests that it has a more red-fruited, earthy profile that Loire geeks might like. I do not think I would call BAMA an archetype Margaux, Julian? I will admit that Margaux is probably the appellation that I have, historically, focused on the least. I have been buying more of it in recent years, as the appellation seems to be firing on all cyclinders.
